title: Romans Passage-Level Natural-Flow Context Packs
publicOutputPolicy: Private editorial support only; source names and research-process language are excluded from public notes.

passages.title: Called and Set Apart for the Gospel
passages.argument: Paul introduces himself through his relation to Christ and the gospel promised in Scripture, centered in the risen Son, and aimed at the obedience of faith among all nations.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul writes before visiting Rome, most likely from Corinth near the close of his third missionary journey. The church in Rome had not been planted by Paul, and it included Jewish and Gentile believers whose life together needed the clear gospel of justification by faith, obedience from faith, and mission to all nations.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 1 opens with Paul's commissioned gospel, turns toward his longing for Rome, states the letter's thesis in Romans 1:16-17, and then lets the darkness of human rebellion show why saving righteousness must come from God.

passages.title: Prayer, Longing, and Gospel Debt
passages.argument: Paul thanks God for the Roman believers, prays to see them, and explains that the gospel entrusted to him has made him a debtor to every kind of person.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul writes before visiting Rome, most likely from Corinth near the close of his third missionary journey. The church in Rome had not been planted by Paul, and it included Jewish and Gentile believers whose life together needed the clear gospel of justification by faith, obedience from faith, and mission to all nations.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 1 opens with Paul's commissioned gospel, turns toward his longing for Rome, states the letter's thesis in Romans 1:16-17, and then lets the darkness of human rebellion show why saving righteousness must come from God.

passages.title: The Power and Righteousness of God
passages.argument: Paul states the center of the letter: the gospel is God's power unto salvation, because in it God's righteousness is revealed and received by faith.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul writes before visiting Rome, most likely from Corinth near the close of his third missionary journey. The church in Rome had not been planted by Paul, and it included Jewish and Gentile believers whose life together needed the clear gospel of justification by faith, obedience from faith, and mission to all nations.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 1 opens with Paul's commissioned gospel, turns toward his longing for Rome, states the letter's thesis in Romans 1:16-17, and then lets the darkness of human rebellion show why saving righteousness must come from God.

passages.title: Truth Suppressed and Worship Exchanged
passages.argument: Paul traces Gentile guilt from rejected light to idolatry, from idolatry to disordered desires, and from disordered desires to a society that approves what God judges.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul writes before visiting Rome, most likely from Corinth near the close of his third missionary journey. The church in Rome had not been planted by Paul, and it included Jewish and Gentile believers whose life together needed the clear gospel of justification by faith, obedience from faith, and mission to all nations.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 1 opens with Paul's commissioned gospel, turns toward his longing for Rome, states the letter's thesis in Romans 1:16-17, and then lets the darkness of human rebellion show why saving righteousness must come from God.

passages.title: God Judges Without Partiality
passages.argument: The one who condemns others still stands under God's searching judgment.
passages.historicalFrame: Jewish believers in Rome prized Scripture, circumcision, and covenant privilege. Paul honors those gifts but refuses to let religious identity replace repentance and faith.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 2 continues the universal indictment. Paul shows that neither moral comparison nor possession of the law can justify sinners before God.

passages.title: Law, Conscience, and Accountability
passages.argument: Gentiles and Jews are judged according to the light they have received.
passages.historicalFrame: Jewish believers in Rome prized Scripture, circumcision, and covenant privilege. Paul honors those gifts but refuses to let religious identity replace repentance and faith.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 2 continues the universal indictment. Paul shows that neither moral comparison nor possession of the law can justify sinners before God.

passages.title: Religious Privilege Cannot Cover Sin
passages.argument: Paul confronts those who boast in the law while dishonoring God by disobedience.
passages.historicalFrame: Jewish believers in Rome prized Scripture, circumcision, and covenant privilege. Paul honors those gifts but refuses to let religious identity replace repentance and faith.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 2 continues the universal indictment. Paul shows that neither moral comparison nor possession of the law can justify sinners before God.

passages.title: Circumcision of the Heart
passages.argument: The true Jew is one inwardly, with praise from God rather than human applause.
passages.historicalFrame: Jewish believers in Rome prized Scripture, circumcision, and covenant privilege. Paul honors those gifts but refuses to let religious identity replace repentance and faith.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 2 continues the universal indictment. Paul shows that neither moral comparison nor possession of the law can justify sinners before God.

passages.title: God Remains True
passages.argument: Human unfaithfulness cannot make God's faithfulness void.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul addresses Jewish and Gentile believers in Rome by honoring Israel's privileges while denying that privilege can justify. The argument treats Scripture as a sacred trust and presses every reader toward the one saving righteousness revealed in Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 3 closes Paul's indictment of humanity and opens the letter's great statement of justification by faith. The chapter moves from objections about Jewish advantage to Scripture's verdict on universal guilt, then to God's saving righteousness in Christ.

passages.title: All the World Guilty Before God
passages.argument: Scripture testifies that Jew and Gentile alike stand under sin.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul addresses Jewish and Gentile believers in Rome by honoring Israel's privileges while denying that privilege can justify. The argument treats Scripture as a sacred trust and presses every reader toward the one saving righteousness revealed in Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 3 closes Paul's indictment of humanity and opens the letter's great statement of justification by faith. The chapter moves from objections about Jewish advantage to Scripture's verdict on universal guilt, then to God's saving righteousness in Christ.

passages.title: Righteousness Through Faith in Christ
passages.argument: God justifies freely through the redemption and atoning work of Jesus.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul addresses Jewish and Gentile believers in Rome by honoring Israel's privileges while denying that privilege can justify. The argument treats Scripture as a sacred trust and presses every reader toward the one saving righteousness revealed in Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 3 closes Paul's indictment of humanity and opens the letter's great statement of justification by faith. The chapter moves from objections about Jewish advantage to Scripture's verdict on universal guilt, then to God's saving righteousness in Christ.

passages.title: Faith Establishes the Law
passages.argument: Boasting is excluded because justification is by faith, yet faith upholds God's law.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul addresses Jewish and Gentile believers in Rome by honoring Israel's privileges while denying that privilege can justify. The argument treats Scripture as a sacred trust and presses every reader toward the one saving righteousness revealed in Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 3 closes Paul's indictment of humanity and opens the letter's great statement of justification by faith. The chapter moves from objections about Jewish advantage to Scripture's verdict on universal guilt, then to God's saving righteousness in Christ.

passages.title: Righteousness Reckoned Apart From Works
passages.argument: Abraham and David witness that God counts righteousness to the believer by grace.
passages.historicalFrame: Abraham was the great covenant ancestor for Jewish identity. Paul uses him to show that God's family is defined by promise and faith before ethnic boundary markers.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 4 illustrates Romans 3:21-31. Paul grounds justification by faith in Scripture itself, especially Genesis 15 and Psalm 32.

passages.title: Faith Before Circumcision
passages.argument: Abraham was counted righteous before receiving the covenant sign.
passages.historicalFrame: Abraham was the great covenant ancestor for Jewish identity. Paul uses him to show that God's family is defined by promise and faith before ethnic boundary markers.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 4 illustrates Romans 3:21-31. Paul grounds justification by faith in Scripture itself, especially Genesis 15 and Psalm 32.

passages.title: Promise by Grace
passages.argument: The promise comes through the righteousness of faith so it may be sure to all the seed.
passages.historicalFrame: Abraham was the great covenant ancestor for Jewish identity. Paul uses him to show that God's family is defined by promise and faith before ethnic boundary markers.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 4 illustrates Romans 3:21-31. Paul grounds justification by faith in Scripture itself, especially Genesis 15 and Psalm 32.

passages.title: Faith in the God Who Raises the Dead
passages.argument: Abraham trusted God's life-giving promise, and believers trust the God who raised Jesus.
passages.historicalFrame: Abraham was the great covenant ancestor for Jewish identity. Paul uses him to show that God's family is defined by promise and faith before ethnic boundary markers.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 4 illustrates Romans 3:21-31. Paul grounds justification by faith in Scripture itself, especially Genesis 15 and Psalm 32.

passages.title: Justification Produces Hope
passages.argument: Peace, access, and hope flow from being justified by faith.
passages.historicalFrame: Roman believers lived under social pressure and imperial claims. Paul gives them a deeper security than status, law, or empire can provide: reconciliation with God through Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 5 bridges justification and new life. It looks back to faith and forward to union with Christ and freedom from sin in chapter 6.

passages.title: Christ Died for the Ungodly
passages.argument: God's love is demonstrated in Christ's death for sinners and enemies.
passages.historicalFrame: Roman believers lived under social pressure and imperial claims. Paul gives them a deeper security than status, law, or empire can provide: reconciliation with God through Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 5 bridges justification and new life. It looks back to faith and forward to union with Christ and freedom from sin in chapter 6.

passages.title: Adam and Christ
passages.argument: Adam's sin brought death, but Christ's obedience brings a greater reign of grace.
passages.historicalFrame: Roman believers lived under social pressure and imperial claims. Paul gives them a deeper security than status, law, or empire can provide: reconciliation with God through Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 5 bridges justification and new life. It looks back to faith and forward to union with Christ and freedom from sin in chapter 6.

passages.title: Buried and Raised With Christ
passages.argument: Baptism points to death to sin and resurrection life with Christ.
passages.historicalFrame: In a world where slaves served masters, Paul uses the language of service to show that everyone belongs somewhere. Grace transfers believers from sin's tyranny to God's liberating rule.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 6 answers a possible objection to Romans 5:20. If grace abounds over sin, should believers continue in sin? Paul answers with union with Christ.

passages.title: Sin Shall Not Have Dominion
passages.argument: Believers must not let sin reign, because they live under grace.
passages.historicalFrame: In a world where slaves served masters, Paul uses the language of service to show that everyone belongs somewhere. Grace transfers believers from sin's tyranny to God's liberating rule.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 6 answers a possible objection to Romans 5:20. If grace abounds over sin, should believers continue in sin? Paul answers with union with Christ.

passages.title: Servants of Righteousness
passages.argument: Everyone serves either sin leading to death or God leading to holiness and life.
passages.historicalFrame: In a world where slaves served masters, Paul uses the language of service to show that everyone belongs somewhere. Grace transfers believers from sin's tyranny to God's liberating rule.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 6 answers a possible objection to Romans 5:20. If grace abounds over sin, should believers continue in sin? Paul answers with union with Christ.

passages.title: Released to Serve in Newness of Spirit
passages.argument: Believers are released from the law's condemning jurisdiction to belong to Christ.
passages.historicalFrame: Jewish and Gentile believers both needed clarity on the law. Paul distinguishes the law's goodness from sin's misuse of the commandment.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 7 follows the call to new obedience in chapter 6 and prepares for the Spirit-filled life of chapter 8.

passages.title: The Law Exposes Sin
passages.argument: The commandment is holy, but sin uses it to reveal human rebellion.
passages.historicalFrame: Jewish and Gentile believers both needed clarity on the law. Paul distinguishes the law's goodness from sin's misuse of the commandment.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 7 follows the call to new obedience in chapter 6 and prepares for the Spirit-filled life of chapter 8.

passages.title: The Struggle and the Deliverer
passages.argument: Paul portrays the misery of willing the good without power to perform it.
passages.historicalFrame: Jewish and Gentile believers both needed clarity on the law. Paul distinguishes the law's goodness from sin's misuse of the commandment.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 7 follows the call to new obedience in chapter 6 and prepares for the Spirit-filled life of chapter 8.

passages.title: No Condemnation and Life by the Spirit
passages.argument: The Spirit applies Christ's victory and frees believers from sin's condemning rule.
passages.historicalFrame: Believers in Rome faced weakness, suffering, and political uncertainty. Paul anchors them in the Spirit's present work and God's future restoration.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 8 crowns the argument of Romans 5-7. It shows the life that justification creates and the assurance that belongs to those united to Christ.

passages.title: Children and Heirs
passages.argument: The Spirit leads believers as adopted children who cry, Abba, Father.
passages.historicalFrame: Believers in Rome faced weakness, suffering, and political uncertainty. Paul anchors them in the Spirit's present work and God's future restoration.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 8 crowns the argument of Romans 5-7. It shows the life that justification creates and the assurance that belongs to those united to Christ.

passages.title: Suffering and Future Glory
passages.argument: Present suffering is real, but creation waits for God's final restoration.
passages.historicalFrame: Believers in Rome faced weakness, suffering, and political uncertainty. Paul anchors them in the Spirit's present work and God's future restoration.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 8 crowns the argument of Romans 5-7. It shows the life that justification creates and the assurance that belongs to those united to Christ.

passages.title: The Spirit's Help and God's Purpose
passages.argument: The Spirit intercedes in weakness and God works toward the believer's final conformity to Christ.
passages.historicalFrame: Believers in Rome faced weakness, suffering, and political uncertainty. Paul anchors them in the Spirit's present work and God's future restoration.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 8 crowns the argument of Romans 5-7. It shows the life that justification creates and the assurance that belongs to those united to Christ.

passages.title: Nothing Can Separate Us
passages.argument: God's love in Christ stands above accusation, danger, and death.
passages.historicalFrame: Believers in Rome faced weakness, suffering, and political uncertainty. Paul anchors them in the Spirit's present work and God's future restoration.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 8 crowns the argument of Romans 5-7. It shows the life that justification creates and the assurance that belongs to those united to Christ.

passages.title: Paul's Sorrow for Israel
passages.argument: Paul grieves over his people and honors their covenant privileges.
passages.historicalFrame: The church's Gentile growth raised painful questions about Israel's place in God's plan. Paul answers with Scripture, grief, and confidence in God's mercy.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 9 begins a three-chapter unit on Israel, the Gentiles, and God's faithfulness to His promises.

passages.title: Children of the Promise
passages.argument: God's promise works through His calling, not through descent alone.
passages.historicalFrame: The church's Gentile growth raised painful questions about Israel's place in God's plan. Paul answers with Scripture, grief, and confidence in God's mercy.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 9 begins a three-chapter unit on Israel, the Gentiles, and God's faithfulness to His promises.

passages.title: Mercy and the Remnant
passages.argument: God is righteous in showing mercy and preserving a remnant according to promise.
passages.historicalFrame: The church's Gentile growth raised painful questions about Israel's place in God's plan. Paul answers with Scripture, grief, and confidence in God's mercy.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 9 begins a three-chapter unit on Israel, the Gentiles, and God's faithfulness to His promises.

passages.title: The Stumbling Stone
passages.argument: Gentiles attain righteousness by faith while unbelief stumbles over Christ.
passages.historicalFrame: The church's Gentile growth raised painful questions about Israel's place in God's plan. Paul answers with Scripture, grief, and confidence in God's mercy.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 9 begins a three-chapter unit on Israel, the Gentiles, and God's faithfulness to His promises.

passages.title: Zeal Must Submit to Christ
passages.argument: Israel's zeal is real but misdirected when it seeks righteousness apart from Christ.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul speaks as a Jewish apostle who knows religious zeal from the inside. He does not mock zeal, but he insists that zeal must submit to God's righteousness in Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 10 answers how Israel has responded to the gospel. It highlights the nearness and simplicity of faith while stressing the necessity of proclamation.

passages.title: The Nearness of Faith
passages.argument: The righteousness of faith receives the word near at hand and calls on the Lord.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul speaks as a Jewish apostle who knows religious zeal from the inside. He does not mock zeal, but he insists that zeal must submit to God's righteousness in Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 10 answers how Israel has responded to the gospel. It highlights the nearness and simplicity of faith while stressing the necessity of proclamation.

passages.title: Faith Comes by Hearing
passages.argument: People call on Christ as the gospel is sent, preached, heard, and believed.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul speaks as a Jewish apostle who knows religious zeal from the inside. He does not mock zeal, but he insists that zeal must submit to God's righteousness in Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 10 answers how Israel has responded to the gospel. It highlights the nearness and simplicity of faith while stressing the necessity of proclamation.

passages.title: Israel Has Heard Yet Resisted
passages.argument: Scripture shows that the message has gone out, while God continues to stretch out His hands.
passages.historicalFrame: Paul speaks as a Jewish apostle who knows religious zeal from the inside. He does not mock zeal, but he insists that zeal must submit to God's righteousness in Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 10 answers how Israel has responded to the gospel. It highlights the nearness and simplicity of faith while stressing the necessity of proclamation.

passages.title: A Remnant According to Grace
passages.argument: God preserves a faithful remnant just as He did in Elijah's day.
passages.historicalFrame: Gentile believers could be tempted to despise unbelieving Jews. Paul warns them against arrogance and reminds them that they stand by faith.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 11 completes the Israel section with warning, hope, and doxology.

passages.title: The Olive Tree Warning
passages.argument: Gentiles are grafted in by faith and must not boast over the branches.
passages.historicalFrame: Gentile believers could be tempted to despise unbelieving Jews. Paul warns them against arrogance and reminds them that they stand by faith.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 11 completes the Israel section with warning, hope, and doxology.

passages.title: Mercy for Jews and Gentiles
passages.argument: God's dealings with Israel and the nations reveal a purpose of mercy.
passages.historicalFrame: Gentile believers could be tempted to despise unbelieving Jews. Paul warns them against arrogance and reminds them that they stand by faith.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 11 completes the Israel section with warning, hope, and doxology.

passages.title: Doxology
passages.argument: Paul ends the mystery with worship before God's wisdom and ways.
passages.historicalFrame: Gentile believers could be tempted to despise unbelieving Jews. Paul warns them against arrogance and reminds them that they stand by faith.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 11 completes the Israel section with warning, hope, and doxology.

passages.title: A Living Sacrifice
passages.argument: Mercy calls for whole-life worship and a renewed mind.
passages.historicalFrame: Roman society prized status and honor. Paul forms a different kind of community where mercy creates humility, service, and enemy-love.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 12 begins the practical section. The imperatives rest on the mercies explained in Romans 1-11.

passages.title: Grace-Gifts in One Body
passages.argument: Believers serve humbly according to gifts given by grace.
passages.historicalFrame: Roman society prized status and honor. Paul forms a different kind of community where mercy creates humility, service, and enemy-love.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 12 begins the practical section. The imperatives rest on the mercies explained in Romans 1-11.

passages.title: Love Without Hypocrisy
passages.argument: Paul sketches practical love, hospitality, humility, peace, and non-retaliation.
passages.historicalFrame: Roman society prized status and honor. Paul forms a different kind of community where mercy creates humility, service, and enemy-love.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 12 begins the practical section. The imperatives rest on the mercies explained in Romans 1-11.

passages.title: Responsible Life Under Authority
passages.argument: Civil authority is treated as a servant of public order, and believers are called to honest civic conduct.
passages.historicalFrame: Christians in Rome lived under imperial power. Paul teaches public order without making the state ultimate, since believers still belong first to Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 13 continues the ethic of transformed life from chapter 12, applying love to civic responsibility and urgent holiness.

passages.title: Love Fulfills the Law
passages.argument: Love does no harm to a neighbor and fulfills the moral intent of God's commandments.
passages.historicalFrame: Christians in Rome lived under imperial power. Paul teaches public order without making the state ultimate, since believers still belong first to Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 13 continues the ethic of transformed life from chapter 12, applying love to civic responsibility and urgent holiness.

passages.title: Awake and Put on Christ
passages.argument: Believers live in the dawn of Christ's return and reject the works of darkness.
passages.historicalFrame: Christians in Rome lived under imperial power. Paul teaches public order without making the state ultimate, since believers still belong first to Christ.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 13 continues the ethic of transformed life from chapter 12, applying love to civic responsibility and urgent holiness.

passages.title: Receive One Another Before God
passages.argument: Believers must not despise or condemn one another over disputable scruples.
passages.historicalFrame: Jewish and Gentile believers carried different scruples about food, calendars, and association. Paul protects conscience while placing love above self-assertion.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 14 applies the gospel to church fellowship. It continues the love ethic of chapters 12-13 in a conflicted congregation.

passages.title: Use Liberty in Love
passages.argument: Freedom must not destroy a brother or sister for whom Christ died.
passages.historicalFrame: Jewish and Gentile believers carried different scruples about food, calendars, and association. Paul protects conscience while placing love above self-assertion.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 14 applies the gospel to church fellowship. It continues the love ethic of chapters 12-13 in a conflicted congregation.

passages.title: Christlike Bearing of the Weak
passages.argument: Believers please their neighbor for edification, following Christ's self-giving pattern.
passages.historicalFrame: The Roman church needed unity across Jewish and Gentile lines. Paul connects mutual welcome with Scripture's hope and the gospel's mission to the nations.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 15 continues the conscience-and-liberty appeal from chapter 14 and transitions into Paul's ministry report, travel plans, and prayer request.

passages.title: Christ Welcomes Jews and Gentiles
passages.argument: Christ confirms God's promises and brings the nations into praise.
passages.historicalFrame: The Roman church needed unity across Jewish and Gentile lines. Paul connects mutual welcome with Scripture's hope and the gospel's mission to the nations.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 15 continues the conscience-and-liberty appeal from chapter 14 and transitions into Paul's ministry report, travel plans, and prayer request.

passages.title: Paul's Priestly Mission
passages.argument: Paul describes his Gentile mission as an offering sanctified by the Spirit.
passages.historicalFrame: The Roman church needed unity across Jewish and Gentile lines. Paul connects mutual welcome with Scripture's hope and the gospel's mission to the nations.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 15 continues the conscience-and-liberty appeal from chapter 14 and transitions into Paul's ministry report, travel plans, and prayer request.

passages.title: Travel Plans and Prayer
passages.argument: Paul shares his plans for Jerusalem, Rome, and Spain while asking for prayer.
passages.historicalFrame: The Roman church needed unity across Jewish and Gentile lines. Paul connects mutual welcome with Scripture's hope and the gospel's mission to the nations.
passages.literaryMovement: Romans 15 continues the conscience-and-liberty appeal from chapter 14 and transitions into Paul's ministry report, travel plans, and prayer request.

passages.title: A Network of Gospel Workers
passages.argument: Paul commends Phoebe and greets many believers who labored in Christ.
passages.historicalFrame: The many names in Romans 16 show a diverse house-church network with women and men serving in costly gospel partnership.
passages.literaryMovement: The closing greetings are not an appendix. They show the embodied community produced by the doctrine of Romans.

passages.title: Discernment Against Division
passages.argument: Paul warns against divisive teaching and promises God's victory over Satan.
passages.historicalFrame: The many names in Romans 16 show a diverse house-church network with women and men serving in costly gospel partnership.
passages.literaryMovement: The closing greetings are not an appendix. They show the embodied community produced by the doctrine of Romans.

passages.title: Companions Send Greetings
passages.argument: Paul's coworkers add their greetings, showing the shared labor behind the letter.
passages.historicalFrame: The many names in Romans 16 show a diverse house-church network with women and men serving in costly gospel partnership.
passages.literaryMovement: The closing greetings are not an appendix. They show the embodied community produced by the doctrine of Romans.

passages.title: Doxology to the Wise God
passages.argument: The letter ends in praise for the gospel now revealed to all nations.
passages.historicalFrame: The many names in Romans 16 show a diverse house-church network with women and men serving in costly gospel partnership.
passages.literaryMovement: The closing greetings are not an appendix. They show the embodied community produced by the doctrine of Romans.
